optimization/encoding: * **Data Encoding Method**: The raw data was pre-processed by integrating and correcting chromatographic peaks using MultiQuant software. The relative abundance of lipid metabolites in plasma was obtained by calculating the peak area of each chromatogram in mass spectrometry data. The signal intensity of the characteristic ions was measured in counts per second. The data quality of lipid metabolites was evaluated using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to assess homogeneity and reproducibility. Orthogonal Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis (OPLS-DA) was applied to remove irrelevant variables, and Variable Importance in Projection (VIP) values were obtained from each lipid variable to measure the contribution of variables to the model. The quality of the OPLS-DA model was validated using a permutation test. The differentially expressed lipid metabolites were determined by a combination of fold change (FC), false discovery rate (FDR), and VIP values.

optimization/fitting:
* **p > N: No**

  The study used a 10-fold cross-validation method to verify the accuracy of the diagnostic model. This method splits the data into 10 parts, using 9 for training and 1 for testing, repeating this process 10 times. This approach ensures that each data point is used for both training and testing, effectively leveraging the entire dataset of N=119 samples. Given that the model is evaluated across multiple folds, the effective parameter count (p) relative to the training set size (N) does not exceed it.

* **Over-fitting Evidence**: Permutation Test

  The quality of the OPLS-DA model was validated by a permutation test, which involves randomly shuffling the class labels and comparing the model's performance on the permuted data. The results showed P < 0.005, indicating that the model was not overfitting and was reliable for screening lipid biomarkers.

evaluation/method:
* **Evaluation Strategy**:
  * 10-fold Cross-Validation: The dataset was divided into 10 parts, with 9 parts used for training and 1 part used for testing. This process was repeated 10 times, with each part used as the test set once. The logistic regression model achieved an AUC of 0.908, 85.3% sensitivity, and 85.9% specificity through this validation method.
